content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:cc6d1ec1

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Past transcript contains durable facts about the user's birding hobby, prior Audubon workshop, learning methods, and planned meetup, plus broader reusable context about beginner birding interests.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        - note hot-path-87501da3537d-note-3: DIRECTION; Bird call recognition approach: start local and practice by ear; For bird-call learning, the user is focusing on common local birds in their backyard, especially the Downy Woodpecker's distinctive high-pitched “pik” call, and practices with phone recordings before applying recognition outdoors.; scope=User's current method for improving bird call recognition within birdwatching
- profile benchmark:longmemeval:cc6d1ec1:profile:<0> Profile for User (USER).
Stable facts:
- The user has a goal of spotting a Red-headed Woodpecker.
- The user wants to improve their bird call recognition skills.
- The user is considering offering suet to attract more Downy Woodpeckers.
- The user has been getting into birdwatching for about three months as of 2023-05-25.
- The user has seen Downy Woodpeckers in their area.
- claim hot-path-87501da3537d-claim-9: has_goal; The user wants to improve their bird call recognition skills.; scope=User's birdwatching learning goals in the imported chat session.
- note hot-path-87501da3537d-note-2: CONTEXT; Birdwatching learning context and backyard focus; The user is a beginner birder building a backyard birding setup: they started birdwatching around March 2023, are setting up feeders including suet, and are using a life list and bird-call practice to improve identification skills.; scope=User's birdwatching hobby context as established in LongMemEval cc6d1ec1 session 1
- claim hot-path-87501da3537d-claim-5: has_experience_with; The user has been getting into birdwatching for about three months as of 2023-05-25.; scope=User's birdwatching hobby context as of the imported 2023-05-25 chat session.
- claim hot-path-87501da3537d-claim-10: current_metric_value; The user's birding life list contains 35 species as of 2023-05-25.; scope=User's birding life-list metric from the imported 2023-05-25 chat session.
- claim hot-path-87501da3537d-claim-8: has_goal; The user is considering offering suet to attract more Downy Woodpeckers.; scope=User's backyard bird-feeding plans in the imported chat session.
- claim hot-path-87501da3537d-claim-7: has_experience_with; The user has seen Downy Woodpeckers in their area.; scope=User's local bird sightings in the imported chat session.
- claim hot-path-87501da3537d-claim-6: has_goal; The user has a goal of spotting a Red-headed Woodpecker.; scope=User's birdwatching goals in the imported chat session.
- note hot-path-87501da3537d-note-4: LESSON; Red-headed Woodpecker spotting strategy differs from suet woodpecker feeding; The user's target species is Red-headed Woodpecker, but the session established that suet is more useful for attracting Downy, Hairy, and Red-bellied Woodpeckers; Red-headed Woodpecker sightings are more likely supported by open habitat, native plants, insect-friendly areas, fruits/berries/seeds, and water.; scope=Birdwatching guidance relevant to the user's Red-headed Woodpecker goal

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I'm / my / I've; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<0>; type=existing; name=I'm / my / I've; reason=First-person statements refer to the stable namespace-level User entity.
- mention=local Audubon society; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Local Audubon Society; reason=Concrete organization mentioned as the place where the user attended a bird watching workshop; no existing matching entity.
- mention=bird watching workshop; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=OTHER; name=Bird Watching Workshop; reason=Concrete event-like referent needed for attended_event; no existing matching entity.
- mention=birder's meetup; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=OTHER; name=Birder's Meetup; reason=Concrete event-like referent mentioned as a planned attendance; no existing matching entity.
- mention=guided walk; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=OTHER; name=Guided Birding Walk; reason=Concrete event/activity likely needed as object of planned participation and learning; distinct from the broader meetup.
- mention=local nature preserve; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=LOCATION; name=Local Nature Preserve; reason=Concrete location mentioned as the venue of the guided walk; no existing matching entity.
- mention=Merlin Bird ID; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=PRODUCT; name=Merlin Bird ID; reason=Named reusable product mentioned as a birding resource; no existing matching entity.
- mention=Cornell Lab of Ornithology; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Cornell Lab of Ornithology; reason=Named reusable organization mentioned as provider of birding resources; no existing matching entity.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|local Audubon society|Audubon workshop|birder's meetup|local nature preserve|birding|bird watching|bird calls|field guide|bird feeder|birdbath
        text_queries=bird watching workshop local Audubon society attended a month ago|beginner birding field guide bird calls recordings on phone|birder meetup next weekend guided walk local nature preserve|backyard bird feeder birdbath attract birds
        predicate_hints=attended_event|has_experience_with|has_goal|prefers

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:
        [external:provided-text:2ffe7cdaf45d511dc530c88732729e98] EXTERNAL
Source metadata: origin=provided_text; input_kind=TEXT; source_ref=longmemeval:cc6d1ec1:session:2; title=LongMemEval cc6d1ec1 session 2; user_consent_confirmed=true; force_memory_write=true; mode=force
LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/25 (Thu) 19:03
Transcript:
user: I'm considering getting a bird feeder for my backyard, but I'm not sure what type of seed to use. Can you recommend a good all-purpose seed mix that will attract a variety of birds? By the way, I recently attended a bird watching workshop at the local Audubon society a month ago, and I learned a lot about attracting birds to my yard.
assistant: That's great to hear that you attended a bird watching workshop! Attracting birds to your yard can be a wonderful hobby.

Choosing the right seed mix is an important part of attracting a variety of birds to your feeder. A good all-purpose seed mix should include a blend of seeds that cater to different bird species and their feeding preferences. Here are some recommendations:

1. **Black oil sunflower seeds**: These are a staple in many bird seed mixes. They're high in fat and protein, making them a favorite among cardinals, finches, and sparrows.
2. **Nyjer seed** (thistle): This tiny seed is rich in oil and preferred by finches, sparrows, and other small birds.
3. **Safflower seeds**: These seeds are a good alternative to sunflower seeds, as they're less likely to attract squirrels and other mammals. They're a favorite among cardinals, finches, and doves.
4. **Suet**: A high-energy food made from animal fat, suet is often mixed with seeds and nuts. It's a great attractant for woodpeckers, nuthatches, and chickadees.
5. **Fruit and nuts**: Adding some dried fruits, like cranberries or cherries, and nuts, like peanuts or hazelnuts, can attract birds like blue jays, woodpeckers, and robins.

When selecting a seed mix, look for a blend that includes a mix of these ingredients. A good all-purpose mix should contain:

* 20-30% black oil sunflower seeds
* 20-30% Nyjer seed
* 20-30% safflower seeds
* 10-20% suet
* 10-20% fruit and nuts

Some popular bird seed brands that offer good all-purpose mixes include:

* Audubon Park
* Perky-Pet
* Wild Birds Unlimited
* Droll Yankees

Remember to choose a seed mix that's fresh and stored properly to ensure the seeds remain viable and attractive to birds.

In addition to the seed mix, consider the following tips to attract a variety of birds to your feeder:

* Place your feeder in an area that receives morning sun and is protected from strong winds and predators.
* Keep your feeder clean and well-maintained to prevent mold and bacteria growth.
* Provide a source of water, like a birdbath or pond, for birds to drink and bathe in.
* Plant native vegetation and flowers that provide shelter, food, and nesting sites for birds.

Enjoy your bird watching hobby, and happy birding!
user: I'm also thinking of getting a birdbath to go along with my feeder. Do you have any recommendations for a birdbath that's easy to clean and maintain?
assistant: A birdbath is a wonderful addition to your backyard, and it's essential to choose one that's easy to clean and maintain to keep the water fresh and safe for the birds.

Here are some recommendations for birdbaths that are easy to clean and maintain:

1. **Shallow birdbaths**: Look for birdbaths with a shallow depth (around 1-2 inches) and a wide basin. This design allows for easy cleaning and prevents debris from accumulating at the bottom.
2. **Smooth surfaces**: Opt for birdbaths with smooth, non-porous surfaces, such as ceramic, glass, or plastic. These materials are easy to clean and resistant to algae growth.
3. **Drainage holes**: Choose a birdbath with built-in drainage holes or a removable plug for easy water changes. This feature helps prevent stagnant water and reduces maintenance.
4. **Easy-to-lift design**: Consider a birdbath with a lightweight and easy-to-lift design, making it simple to clean and refill.

Some popular birdbath brands that fit these criteria include:

* **Bird Brain**: Known for their innovative designs and easy-to-clean birdbaths, Bird Brain offers a range of options with smooth surfaces and drainage holes.
* **Perky-Pet**: Perky-Pet offers a variety of birdbaths with shallow depths, smooth surfaces, and easy-to-lift designs.
* **Droll Yankees**: Droll Yankees provides high-quality birdbaths with features like built-in drainage holes and smooth, non-porous surfaces.

When selecting a birdbath, also consider the following tips:

* **Size**: Choose a birdbath that's large enough to accommodate the number of birds you expect to visit, but not so large that it's difficult to clean.
* **Material**: Avoid birdbaths made from materials that can harbor bacteria, such as concrete or wood.
* **Placement**: Position your birdbath in a spot that receives partial shade to reduce algae growth and prevent water from becoming too hot.
* **Maintenance**: Clean your birdbath regularly by scrubbing it with a soft brush and mild soap, and refilling it with fresh water every 2-3 days.

By choosing a birdbath that's easy to clean and maintain, you'll be able to provide a safe and healthy environment for the birds to enjoy.

Remember, a clean birdbath is essential for the health and well-being of the birds. Regular cleaning and maintenance will help prevent the spread of diseases and keep your backyard birds happy and healthy!
user: I'm also thinking of getting a field guide to learn more about the different bird species and their habits. Do you have any recommendations for a field guide that's good for beginners?
assistant: A field guide is an excellent resource for learning about bird species and their habits. As a beginner, you'll want a guide that's easy to use, comprehensive, and filled with helpful information. Here are some recommendations for field guides that are perfect for beginners:

1. **National Audubon Society Field Guide to Birds**: This is a classic field guide that's considered a staple for bird enthusiasts. It covers over 800 species, with detailed descriptions, range maps, and excellent photographs.
2. **Sibley Guide to Birds**: Written and illustrated by David Allen Sibley, this guide is renowned for its comprehensive coverage of over 900 species. It's a bit more detailed than the Audubon guide, but still accessible to beginners.
3. **Peterson Field Guide to Birds**: This guide has been a trusted resource for over 70 years, covering over 750 species. It's known for its clear descriptions, range maps, and illustrations.
4. **Kaufman Field Guide to Birds**: This guide is written by Kenn Kaufman, a well-known ornithologist, and covers over 750 species. It's known for its user-friendly layout, excellent photographs, and helpful tips for identification.

When choosing a field guide, consider the following factors:

* **Region**: If you're only interested in birds found in a specific region, look for a guide that focuses on that area (e.g., Eastern or Western North America).
* **Level of detail**: If you're a complete beginner, you might prefer a guide with simpler descriptions and more illustrations. If you're looking for more in-depth information, a guide like the Sibley Guide might be a better fit.
* **Photographs vs. illustrations**: Some guides feature photographs, while others have illustrations. Both have their advantages, but photographs can be more helpful for identification.
* **Size and portability**: If you plan to take your field guide with you on birding trips, consider a smaller, more portable option.

In addition to a field guide, you might also consider the following resources to help you learn more about birds:

* **Mobile apps**: Apps like Merlin Bird ID, iBird Pro, or Audubon Birds can help you identify birds using photos and descriptions.
* **Online resources**: Websites like AllAboutBirds, BirdLife International, or the Cornell Lab of Ornithology offer a wealth of information on bird species, habits, and conservation.
* **Local birding groups**: Joining a local birding group or attending birding events can connect you with experienced birders who can offer guidance and advice.

Remember, the key to becoming a skilled birder is to practice, observe, and learn from your experiences. A field guide is just the starting point, and you'll find that it becomes a trusted companion as you explore the world of birds!
user: I've been meaning to learn more about bird calls, and I've been listening to recordings on my phone to help me identify birds by ear. Do you have any tips on how to improve my skills in this area?
assistant: Learning bird calls is a fantastic way to enhance your birding skills! Recognizing birds by their vocalizations can be a game-changer, especially when you're in the field and can't get a good visual ID. Here are some tips to help you improve your skills:

1. **Start with common birds**: Begin with familiar species that you regularly see in your area. Focus on learning the calls of birds like American Robin, Northern Cardinal, Blue Jay, or Mourning Dove.
2. **Listen to recordings repeatedly**: Familiarize yourself with the recordings, and listen to them multiple times. Try to identify the bird call without looking at the species name or description.
3. **Practice active listening**: When you're outdoors, actively listen for bird calls. Pay attention to the pitch, tone, rhythm, and volume of the calls. Try to mimic the calls in your mind or out loud.
4. **Compare similar species**: Learn to distinguish between similar-sounding species. For example, the calls of the Chipping Sparrow and the Clay-colored Sparrow are similar, but not identical.
5. **Use visual aids**: Watch videos or animations that show the bird's beak movement, posture, or other visual cues that can help you associate with the call.
6. **Learn bird call patterns**: Many birds have distinctive call patterns, such as the series of notes or the repetition of a particular phrase. Recognizing these patterns can help you identify birds more quickly.
7. **Join a birding group or find a mentor**: Participate in guided bird walks or join online forums where you can learn from experienced birders. They can provide valuable insights and help you improve your skills.
8. **Use apps and online resources**: There are many excellent apps, like Merlin Bird ID or iBird Pro, that offer interactive bird call lessons and quizzes. You can also find online resources, such as birding websites or YouTube channels, that provide bird call tutorials.
9. **Be patient and persistent**: Learning bird calls takes time and practice. Don't get discouraged if you struggle at first. Keep listening, and your skills will improve over time.
10. **Take notes and review**: Keep a journal or spreadsheet to note the bird calls you've learned and the ones you're still struggling with. Review your notes regularly to reinforce your learning.

Some popular resources for learning bird calls include:

* The Cornell Lab of Ornithology's All About Birds website and Merlin Bird ID app
* The Audubon Society's Birds of a Feather app
* The Sibley Guide to Birds app
* Xeno-canto, a comprehensive online database of bird calls and sounds
* Birding websites and forums, such as BirdForum or Reddit's r/birding

Remember, the key to improving your bird call identification skills is consistent practice and exposure to a variety of bird species. Happy birding!
user: I've been thinking about attending a birder's meetup next weekend, where we'll be going on a guided walk at a local nature preserve.
assistant: That sounds like a great idea! Attending a birder's meetup is an excellent way to connect with like-minded people, learn from experienced birders, and gain hands-on experience in the field.

Here are a few tips to make the most out of your experience:

1. **Dress appropriately**: Wear comfortable clothing and shoes suitable for walking, and dress in layers for changing weather conditions. Don't forget to bring sun protection, insect repellent, and a hat.
2. **Bring binoculars and a field guide**: Make sure your binoculars are in good condition, and consider bringing a field guide to help you identify birds.
3. **Arrive early**: Get to the meetup location a little early to introduce yourself to the group leader and other participants. This is a great opportunity to ask questions and get a sense of the group's dynamics.
4. **Be prepared to learn**: Come with an open mind and a willingness to learn. Don't be afraid to ask questions or seek clarification on bird identifications.
5. **Respect the environment and other birders**: Remember to stay on designated trails, avoid disturbing wildlife, and keep noise levels down to minimize disturbance to the birds and other participants.
6. **Take notes and photos**: Bring a notebook and pencil to jot down notes on the birds you see, and consider taking photos to help with identification later.
7. **Enjoy the experience**: Above all, have fun! Birding is a hobby that's meant to be enjoyed, so take time to appreciate the surroundings, the company, and the thrill of spotting new species.

Some questions to ask the group leader or other participants during the walk:

* What's the best way to identify this species?
* Can you tell me more about the habitat and behavior of this bird?
* How do you distinguish between similar species?
* What's the most interesting or unusual birding experience you've had?

By following these tips, you'll be well-prepared for a fun and educational experience at the birder's meetup. Enjoy your walk and happy birding!
user: I'm looking forward to the meetup and learning more about the local bird species. I've been trying to get out more to spot some birds, and this guided walk will be a great opportunity to see some new species.
assistant: That's the right attitude! It's great that you're enthusiastic about birding and eager to learn more. A guided walk with experienced birders is an excellent way to spot new species and gain valuable insights from those who know the area and its birds.

Remember to be mindful of your surroundings and the birds you're observing. Respect their habitat, and keep a safe distance to avoid disturbing them. It's essential to prioritize the well-being of the birds and the environment while enjoying your hobby.

Don't hesitate to ask questions or seek guidance from the group leader or other participants. They can provide valuable tips on identification, habitat, and behavior, which will enhance your overall birding experience.

Take your time, and enjoy the process of observing and learning. Birding is a hobby that requires patience, and it's essential to slow down and appreciate the beauty of nature.

Keep in mind that birding is not just about spotting birds; it's also about connecting with nature, exercising, and socializing with like-minded people. So, be sure to take in the surroundings, enjoy the company of your fellow birders, and have fun!

I hope you have a fantastic time on your guided walk and spot some amazing birds!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.

Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Past transcript contains durable facts about the user's birding hobby, prior Audubon workshop, learning methods, and planned meetup, plus broader reusable context about beginner birding interests.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm considering getting a bird feeder for my backyard, but I'm not sure what type of seed to use. Can you recommend a good all-purpose seed mix that will attract a variety of birds? By the way, I recently attended a bird watching workshop at the local Audubon society a month ago, and I learned a lot about attracting birds to my yard.
assistant: That's great to hear that you attended a bird watching workshop! Attracting birds to your yard can be a wonderful hobby.

Choosing the right seed mix is an important part of attracting a variety of birds to your feeder. A good all-purpose seed mix should include a blend of seeds that cater to different bird species and their feeding preferences. Here are some recommendations:

1. **Black oil sunflower seeds**: These are a staple in many bird seed mixes. They're high in fat and protein, making them a favorite among cardinals, finches, and sparrows.
2. **Nyjer seed** (thistle): This tiny seed is rich in oil and preferred by finches, sparrows, and other small birds.
3. **Safflower seeds**: These seeds are a good alternative to sunflower seeds, as they're less likely to attract squirrels and other mammals. They're a favorite among cardinals, finches, and doves.
4. **Suet**: A high-energy food made from animal fat, suet is often mixed with seeds and nuts. It's a great attractant for woodpeckers, nuthatches, and chickadees.
5. **Fruit and nuts**: Adding some dried fruits, like cranberries or cherries, and nuts, like peanuts or hazelnuts, can attract birds like blue jays, woodpeckers, and robins.

When selecting a seed mix, look for a blend that includes a mix of these ingredients. A good all-purpose mix should contain:

* 20-30% black oil sunflower seeds
* 20-30% Nyjer seed
* 20-30% safflower seeds
* 10-20% suet
* 10-20% fruit and nuts

Some popular bird seed brands that offer good all-purpose mixes include:

* Audubon Park
* Perky-Pet
* Wild Birds Unlimited
* Droll Yankees

Remember to choose a seed mix that's fresh and stored properly to ensure the seeds remain viable and attractive to birds.

In addition to the seed mix, consider the following tips to attract a variety of birds to your feeder:

* Place your feeder in an area that receives morning sun and is protected from strong winds and predators.
* Keep your feeder clean and well-maintained to prevent mold and bacteria growth.
* Provide a source of water, like a birdbath or pond, for birds to drink and bathe in.
* Plant native vegetation and flowers that provide shelter, food, and nesting sites for birds.

Enjoy your bird watching hobby, and happy birding!
user: I'm also thinking of getting a birdbath to go along with my feeder. Do you have any recommendations for a birdbath that's easy to clean and maintain?
assistant: A birdbath is a wonderful addition to your backyard, and it's essential to choose one that's easy to clean and maintain to keep the water fresh and safe for the birds.

Here are some recommendations for birdbaths that are easy to clean and maintain:

1. **Shallow birdbaths**: Look for birdbaths with a shallow depth (around 1-2 inches) and a wide basin. This design allows for easy cleaning and prevents debris from accumulating at the bottom.
2. **Smooth surfaces**: Opt for birdbaths with smooth, non-porous surfaces, such as ceramic, glass, or plastic. These materials are easy to clean and resistant to algae growth.
3. **Drainage holes**: Choose a birdbath with built-in drainage holes or a removable plug for easy water changes. This feature helps prevent stagnant water and reduces maintenance.
4. **Easy-to-lift design**: Consider a birdbath with a lightweight and easy-to-lift design, making it simple to clean and refill.

Some popular birdbath brands that fit these criteria include:

* **Bird Brain**: Known for their innovative designs and easy-to-clean birdbaths, Bird Brain offers a range of options with smooth surfaces and drainage holes.
* **Perky-Pet**: Perky-Pet offers a variety of birdbaths with shallow depths, smooth surfaces, and easy-to-lift designs.
* **Droll Yankees**: Droll Yankees provides high-quality birdbaths with features like built-in drainage holes and smooth, non-porous surfaces.

When selecting a birdbath, also consider the following tips:

* **Size**: Choose a birdbath that's large enough to accommodate the number of birds you expect to visit, but not so large that it's difficult to clean.
* **Material**: Avoid birdbaths made from materials that can harbor bacteria, such as concrete or wood.
* **Placement**: Position your birdbath in a spot that receives partial shade to reduce algae growth and prevent water from becoming too hot.
* **Maintenance**: Clean your birdbath regularly by scrubbing it with a soft brush and mild soap, and refilling it with fresh water every 2-3 days.

By choosing a birdbath that's easy to clean and maintain, you'll be able to provide a safe and healthy environment for the birds to enjoy.

Remember, a clean birdbath is essential for the health and well-being of the birds. Regular cleaning and maintenance will help prevent the spread of diseases and keep your backyard birds happy and healthy!
user: I'm also thinking of getting a field guide to learn more about the different bird species and their habits. Do you have any recommendations for a field guide that's good for beginners?
assistant: A field guide is an excellent resource for learning about bird species and their habits. As a beginner, you'll want a guide that's easy to use, comprehensive, and filled with helpful information. Here are some recommendations for field guides that are perfect for beginners:

1. **National Audubon Society Field Guide to Birds**: This is a classic field guide that's considered a staple for bird enthusiasts. It covers over 800 species, with detailed descriptions, range maps, and excellent photographs.
2. **Sibley Guide to Birds**: Written and illustrated by David Allen Sibley, this guide is renowned for its comprehensive coverage of over 900 species. It's a bit more detailed than the Audubon guide, but still accessible to beginners.
3. **Peterson Field Guide to Birds**: This guide has been a trusted resource for over 70 years, covering over 750 species. It's known for its clear descriptions, range maps, and illustrations.
4. **Kaufman Field Guide to Birds**: This guide is written by Kenn Kaufman, a well-known ornithologist, and covers over 750 species. It's known for its user-friendly layout, excellent photographs, and helpful tips for identification.

When choosing a field guide, consider the following factors:

* **Region**: If you're only interested in birds found in a specific region, look for a guide that focuses on that area (e.g., Eastern or Western North America).
* **Level of detail**: If you're a complete beginner, you might prefer a guide with simpler descriptions and more illustrations. If you're looking for more in-depth information, a guide like the Sibley Guide might be a better fit.
* **Photographs vs. illustrations**: Some guides feature photographs, while others have illustrations. Both have their advantages, but photographs can be more helpful for identification.
* **Size and portability**: If you plan to take your field guide with you on birding trips, consider a smaller, more portable option.

In addition to a field guide, you might also consider the following resources to help you learn more about birds:

* **Mobile apps**: Apps like Merlin Bird ID, iBird Pro, or Audubon Birds can help you identify birds using photos and descriptions.
* **Online resources**: Websites like AllAboutBirds, BirdLife International, or the Cornell Lab of Ornithology offer a wealth of information on bird species, habits, and conservation.
* **Local birding groups**: Joining a local birding group or attending birding events can connect you with experienced birders who can offer guidance and advice.

Remember, the key to becoming a skilled birder is to practice, observe, and learn from your experiences. A field guide is just the starting point, and you'll find that it becomes a trusted companion as you explore the world of birds!
user: I've been meaning to learn more about bird calls, and I've been listening to recordings on my phone to help me identify birds by ear. Do you have any tips on how to improve my skills in this area?
assistant: Learning bird calls is a fantastic way to enhance your birding skills! Recognizing birds by their vocalizations can be a game-changer, especially when you're in the field and can't get a good visual ID. Here are some tips to help you improve your skills:

1. **Start with common birds**: Begin with familiar species that you regularly see in your area. Focus on learning the calls of birds like American Robin, Northern Cardinal, Blue Jay, or Mourning Dove.
2. **Listen to recordings repeatedly**: Familiarize yourself with the recordings, and listen to them multiple times. Try to identify the bird call without looking at the species name or description.
3. **Practice active listening**: When you're outdoors, actively listen for bird calls. Pay attention to the pitch, tone, rhythm, and volume of the calls. Try to mimic the calls in your mind or out loud.
4. **Compare similar species**: Learn to distinguish between similar-sounding species. For example, the calls of the Chipping Sparrow and the Clay-colored Sparrow are similar, but not identical.
5. **Use visual aids**: Watch videos or animations that show the bird's beak movement, posture, or other visual cues that can help you associate with the call.
6. **Learn bird call patterns**: Many birds have distinctive call patterns, such as the series of notes or the repetition of a particular phrase. Recognizing these patterns can help you identify birds more quickly.
7. **Join a birding group or find a mentor**: Participate in guided bird walks or join online forums where you can learn from experienced birders. They can provide valuable insights and help you improve your skills.
8. **Use apps and online resources**: There are many excellent apps, like Merlin Bird ID or iBird Pro, that offer interactive bird call lessons and quizzes. You can also find online resources, such as birding websites or YouTube channels, that provide bird call tutorials.
9. **Be patient and persistent**: Learning bird calls takes time and practice. Don't get discouraged if you struggle at first. Keep listening, and your skills will improve over time.
10. **Take notes and review**: Keep a journal or spreadsheet to note the bird calls you've learned and the ones you're still struggling with. Review your notes regularly to reinforce your learning.

Some popular resources for learning bird calls include:

* The Cornell Lab of Ornithology's All About Birds website and Merlin Bird ID app
* The Audubon Society's Birds of a Feather app
* The Sibley Guide to Birds app
* Xeno-canto, a comprehensive online database of bird calls and sounds
* Birding websites and forums, such as BirdForum or Reddit's r/birding

Remember, the key to improving your bird call identification skills is consistent practice and exposure to a variety of bird species. Happy birding!
user: I've been thinking about attending a birder's meetup next weekend, where we'll be going on a guided walk at a local nature preserve.
assistant: That sounds like a great idea! Attending a birder's meetup is an excellent way to connect with like-minded people, learn from experienced birders, and gain hands-on experience in the field.

Here are a few tips to make the most out of your experience:

1. **Dress appropriately**: Wear comfortable clothing and shoes suitable for walking, and dress in layers for changing weather conditions. Don't forget to bring sun protection, insect repellent, and a hat.
2. **Bring binoculars and a field guide**: Make sure your binoculars are in good condition, and consider bringing a field guide to help you identify birds.
3. **Arrive early**: Get to the meetup location a little early to introduce yourself to the group leader and other participants. This is a great opportunity to ask questions and get a sense of the group's dynamics.
4. **Be prepared to learn**: Come with an open mind and a willingness to learn. Don't be afraid to ask questions or seek clarification on bird identifications.
5. **Respect the environment and other birders**: Remember to stay on designated trails, avoid disturbing wildlife, and keep noise levels down to minimize disturbance to the birds and other participants.
6. **Take notes and photos**: Bring a notebook and pencil to jot down notes on the birds you see, and consider taking photos to help with identification later.
7. **Enjoy the experience**: Above all, have fun! Birding is a hobby that's meant to be enjoyed, so take time to appreciate the surroundings, the company, and the thrill of spotting new species.

Some questions to ask the group leader or other participants during the walk:

* What's the best way to identify this species?
* Can you tell me more about the habitat and behavior of this bird?
* How do you distinguish between similar species?
* What's the most interesting or unusual birding experience you've had?

By following these tips, you'll be well-prepared for a fun and educational experience at the birder's meetup. Enjoy your walk and happy birding!
user: I'm looking forward to the meetup and learning more about the local bird species. I've been trying to get out more to spot some birds, and this guided walk will be a great opportunity to see some new species.
assistant: That's the right attitude! It's great that you're enthusiastic about birding and eager to learn more. A guided walk with experienced birders is an excellent way to spot new species and gain valuable insights from those who know the area and its birds.

Remember to be mindful of your surroundings and the birds you're observing. Respect their habitat, and keep a safe distance to avoid disturbing them. It's essential to prioritize the well-being of the birds and the environment while enjoying your hobby.

Don't hesitate to ask questions or seek guidance from the group leader or other participants. They can provide valuable tips on identification, habitat, and behavior, which will enhance your overall birding experience.

Take your time, and enjoy the process of observing and learning. Birding is a hobby that requires patience, and it's essential to slow down and appreciate the beauty of nature.

Keep in mind that birding is not just about spotting birds; it's also about connecting with nature, exercising, and socializing with like-minded people. So, be sure to take in the surroundings, enjoy the company of your fellow birders, and have fun!

I hope you have a fantastic time on your guided walk and spot some amazing birds!
